neighborhoods.name: Downtown / Main Street Commercial District
primary_groups: African American merchants, professionals, and tradespeople
neighborhoods.character: Boley's Main Street was the commercial heart of the most prominent all-Black town in America. By 1911, the district had two banks (the Farmers and Merchants Bank and the Boley Bank and Trust Company), a hotel, dry goods stores, grocery stores, a drugstore, a cotton gin, a lumberyard, restaurants, and a newspaper office. Every business was Black-owned. Every customer was Black. The town marshal was Black. The mayor was Black. The postmaster was Black. For people who had come from places where a Black man could not walk on the sidewalk if a white man was approaching, Main Street in Boley was a revolution made out of storefronts and boardwalks.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} walked down Main Street in Boley around {year}, every face was Black, every business was Black-owned, and the law was administered by a Black marshal — a thing that existed almost nowhere else in America.

neighborhoods.name: Residential Neighborhoods and Church Districts
primary_groups: African American families — farmers, laborers, and their families from Southern states
neighborhoods.character: Boley's residential areas radiated outward from the commercial district, organized around the town's churches. Frame houses on modest lots lined unpaved streets. Gardens, chicken coops, and hog pens occupied backyards — this was an agricultural town, and even in-town residents grew food. The churches were the anchors: Methodist, Baptist, and African Methodist Episcopal congregations each claimed a neighborhood. Sunday was the week's social peak, when farm families from surrounding homesteads came to town for services, shopping, and community.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The {surname} family's house in Boley was probably a frame cottage near one of the churches — modest by any measure, but it sat on land they owned in a town they governed, and for a family one generation from {origin_state}, that was not modest at all.

neighborhoods.name: Surrounding Farmsteads and Allotments
primary_groups: African American farming families, Creek Freedmen allottees
neighborhoods.character: Boley sat within the Creek Nation, and the surrounding farmland included allotments held by Creek Freedmen — formerly enslaved people of the Creek Nation who received allotments under the Dawes Act. Black settlers from Southern states purchased land adjacent to these allotments or acquired surplus Creek land. The farming was subsistence and cotton: corn, sweet potatoes, peanuts, and hogs for the table; cotton for cash. The land was better than western Oklahoma — more rain, deeper soil — but still marginal compared to the Delta or East Texas bottomland that many settlers had left.
key_institutions: Rural schools (Rosenwald Fund schools in the 1920s)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The 160 acres that {ancestor_name} farmed outside Boley grew cotton and corn in soil that was not as rich as what {pronoun_subject} had left in {origin_state} — but the difference was that nobody else decided what happened on those acres.

major_employers.name: Cotton Farming (Owner-Operators and Tenants)
major_employers.wages: Owner-operators on 80-160 acre farms might clear $100-400/year after expenses in a good cotton year. Hired farm laborers earned $0.50-1.00/day. Cotton picking paid $0.40-0.75/100 lbs during harvest. The economic margin was thin, and a single drought or price collapse could wipe out a year's work.
major_employers.working_conditions: Farming around Boley was hard, physical labor on land that was productive but not generous. Black farmers here had what most Black farmers in the South did not — title to their own land — but they faced the same commodity price volatility, weather risk, and limited access to credit that plagued all small farmers. The Farmers and Merchants Bank in Boley provided credit to Black farmers who would have been turned away by white-owned banks, but the bank's resources were limited.
major_employers.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name} farmed {pronoun_possessive} own land near Boley — the same backbreaking work as sharecropping in {origin_state}, but with one difference that changed everything: at the end of the year, the crop belonged to the person who grew it.

major_employers.name: Small Businesses (Black-Owned)
major_employers.wages: Highly variable. Merchants' income depended on the agricultural economy — when cotton paid, stores prospered. The bank presidents and newspaper editor were among the town's most prominent citizens. Most business owners also farmed or held side occupations.
major_employers.working_conditions: Boley's Black-owned businesses served an exclusively Black customer base. This was both the point and the limitation: the town demonstrated that Black enterprise could sustain a complete community, but the customer base was small and dependent on a single-crop agricultural economy. When cotton prices collapsed or drought struck, every business on Main Street felt it. The town's isolation from larger markets was both protection (from white economic competition) and constraint (limited access to wholesale goods and capital).
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} ran a business on Boley's Main Street, {pronoun_subject} was part of an experiment in Black economic independence that Booker T. Washington himself came to see — a town where every dollar spent in a Black-owned store stayed in a Black community.

major_employers.name: Fort Smith and Western Railroad (Section Work)
major_employers.wages: Section hands earned $1.00-1.75/day (1900s-1910s). The work was steady but physically punishing.
major_employers.working_conditions: Boley was founded along the Fort Smith and Western Railroad line, and the railroad provided both the town's connection to outside markets and a source of wage employment for men who needed cash income while establishing their farms. Section work — maintaining and repairing track — was available year-round, unlike the seasonal agricultural labor that dominated the local economy. The railroad also employed Black men from Boley as porters and freight handlers at larger stations along the line.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The railroad that created Boley also employed its men — if {ancestor_name} worked a section crew for the Fort Smith and Western, the daily wage kept the family going while the cotton was still in the ground.

major_employers.name: Creek-Seminole College and Agricultural Institute / Boley Public Schools
major_employers.wages: Black teachers in Oklahoma earned $25-50/month (1900s-1910s), considerably less than white teachers. Principals earned slightly more. Despite low pay, teaching was one of the most respected professions in the community.
major_employers.working_conditions: Education was central to Boley's identity. The town's schools were entirely Black-staffed and Black-governed — a source of fierce community pride. Creek-Seminole College (later Creek-Seminole College and Agricultural Institute) provided higher education and agricultural training. The schools were chronically underfunded compared to white schools in Oklahoma, but the community supplemented public funding through church fundraising and private donations. Rosenwald Fund grants helped build improved school buildings in the 1920s.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The schoolteacher in Boley was somebody — if {ancestor_name} taught at the Boley school or Creek-Seminole College, {pronoun_subject} was educating children whose parents had been denied literacy, and that was understood as a calling, not just a job.

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Early settlers built frame houses using lumber shipped in by the Fort Smith and Western Railroad. Some families lived in tents or temporary shelters while building. Town lots were inexpensive, and the town promoters — particularly Thomas Haynes and Lake Moore — sold lots specifically to Black buyers. The earliest buildings were simple box-frame structures, one or two rooms, built by the occupants with help from neighbors. Within two years, the commercial district had proper storefronts with false fronts in the Western tradition.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Boley was an all-Black town by design and by law. There was no ethnic clustering in the conventional sense because the entire community was African American. Internal social geography organized around churches (Baptist, Methodist, AME) and around the distinction between town residents and surrounding farming families. Creek Freedmen allottees on surrounding land occupied a distinct but connected community — they had been in the territory longer than the Southern migrants and had different legal standing as tribal allottees.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Tent or temporary shelter (first weeks) to frame house (first year) to improved frame or brick-trimmed house (3-10 years). Homeownership was nearly universal among established families because the entire point of Boley was Black land ownership. The trajectory stalled or reversed during the agricultural depression of the 1920s and the broader Great Depression, when many families lost land to tax sales or bank failure.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Boley's residents faced hostility not as immigrants but as Black people asserting political and economic independence in a state that was rapidly imposing Jim Crow. Oklahoma statehood in 1907 brought segregation laws that targeted the autonomy of all-Black towns. The broader political climate sought to restrict Black voting, Black land ownership, and Black self-governance — the very things that defined Boley's existence.
specific_incidents: Oklahoma's first act after statehood (1907) was Senate Bill 1, mandating racial segregation on railroads — a direct attack on the mobility of Black citizens
Grandfather clause in the Oklahoma Constitution (1910) designed to disenfranchise Black voters while exempting illiterate white voters
Attempted armed robbery of Boley's bank in 1932 by the Pretty Boy Floyd gang — the town's armed citizens killed two of the robbers, demonstrating the community's capacity for self-defense
Economic strangulation through denial of credit from white-controlled banks and discriminatory agricultural policies that disadvantaged Black farmers
nativist_organizations: Ku Klux Klan (active throughout Oklahoma in the 1920s)
Various white supremacist political organizations pushing Jim Crow legislation at the state level

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: First Baptist Church of Boley
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community — the largest congregation in Boley
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism and marriage records from the town's founding. The church served as both a spiritual center and a civic meeting hall. Records held by the church; some materials at the Oklahoma Historical Society.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: First Baptist was where Boley gathered — if {ancestor_name} joined this congregation, the membership rolls connect the {surname} family to the founding generation of one of America's most remarkable experiments in Black self-governance.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Boley Masonic Lodge (Prince Hall Masons)
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American men — provided insurance, burial benefits, and fraternal fellowship
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership applications with occupation, birthplace, and family details. Lodge records document community leadership networks.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Prince Hall Masons in Boley were the town's leadership network — if {ancestor_name} was a member, the lodge records document not just fraternal membership but the civic connections that ran Boley's schools, businesses, and government.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Farmers and Merchants Bank
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Black farmers and business owners in Boley and surrounding communities
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Bank records are largely lost, but the bank's existence is documented in the Boley Progress newspaper and in Oklahoma banking commission records at the Oklahoma Historical Society.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Farmers and Merchants Bank did something no white bank in Oklahoma would do — it lent money to Black farmers. If {ancestor_name} borrowed from this bank to buy seed or improve the farm, the transaction was an act of community solidarity as much as commerce.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Boley Progress (Newspaper)
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Partial runs of the Boley Progress are held by the Oklahoma Historical Society. The newspaper documented town governance, social events, business activity, agricultural news, and correspondence from the broader Black community. Individual issues may name settlers, record property transactions, and document community events.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Boley Progress reported on everything from cotton prices to church suppers — if the {surname} family appears in its pages, the newspaper provides a week-by-week record of Black community life that few other sources can match.

source_references.author: Norman L. Crockett
source_references.title: The Black Towns
source_references.notes: The foundational academic study of all-Black towns in the American West, with substantial coverage of Boley and other Oklahoma towns. Documents the founding, governance, economy, and decline of these communities within the broader context of Black Western migration.

source_references.author: Hannibal B. Johnson
source_references.title: Acres of Aspiration: The All-Black Towns in Oklahoma
source_references.notes: Comprehensive history of Oklahoma's all-Black towns with detailed profiles of individual communities including Boley. Draws on oral histories, newspaper archives, and community records to document the lived experience of Black town-building.

source_references.author: Booker T. Washington
source_references.title: Boley: A Negro Town in the West
source_references.notes: Primary source article published in The Outlook magazine following Washington's 1905 visit to Boley. Washington praised Boley as a model of Black self-help and economic independence. The article brought national attention to the town and remains one of the most significant contemporary descriptions of an all-Black town at its peak.

source_references.author: Kendra Field
source_references.title: Growing Up with the Country: Family, Race, and Nation after the Civil War
source_references.notes: Scholarly study of Black migration to Indian Territory and Oklahoma, centering the experience of formerly enslaved people and their descendants. Provides essential context for understanding Boley within the broader pattern of Black community-building in the post-Reconstruction era.
